Bonjour Adam,  

L’expérience client ne se résume plus à un sourire. Elle est une dimension complexe et essentielle de la rentabilité, dans un monde d’attentes changeantes et d’abondance de données utilisées et inutilisées. 

Le 2 juin : apprenez-en plus sur comment transformer l’expérience des clients de votre entreprise grâce à des approches et technologies prédictives basées sur des données sans perdre de vue vos fondations. 

Nous comprenons les pressions que vous vivez et qui pèsent sur vos décisions de CX : l'inflation réduit les marges et la rareté de main-d'œuvre fragilise le service, mais les exigences des clients grimpent sans fin. 
👉 Je m'inscris!
 
Venez obtenir des réponses concrètes à trois défis de performance : 
Émotion | Comment utiliser l’IA pour personnaliser l’expérience d’achat des humains?
Action | Comment exploiter avec respect les données liées aux émotions de vos clients?
Culture | Comment former vos équipes pour que l'ADN et l’authenticité de votre CX soit protégée?

Échangez avec des expert.es et des leaders de l’expérience client en entreprise qui ont pris ce chemin. Repartez avec une feuille de route claire, loin de la théorie abstraite, à mettre en œuvre tout de suite. 
 
🚢 Profitez de notre offre pour la croisière conférence CX :

Subject: Re: Smart - Scale - Ticket 12028 - Smart Scale - SQL Server License Verification

Hi All,

Apologies for the delay, I'm ill and I think Jason has also been out.

Samar has confirmed that we do not need SA in this specific scenario, and during our next SPLA audit we should supply the MS auditor with the following:
  • Invoices of their SQL server and CAL license purchases (I think we have these already).
  • A signed legal letter that states that the customer knows they are responsible for making sure they have the correct number of SQL CALs, and are liable for any and all fines that Microsoft make on their behalf. I don't think this is strictly needed as Samar from Insight said "they won't chase you for this", but she said it would be good to have. Anyone we can get from legal to do this?
The only other concern is that they have only purchased 1x User/Device CAL for each server. It's extremely unlikely (but not impossible) that there is only 1 user/device that is using each SQL server, so we should go back and ask them to clarify exactly how many users/devices they are actually using. I think that they are assuming they only need to license the one server with the CAL and not the users that then access it, but they actually have to license each end user/device that they are using. Sharing the one server is called "Multiplexing" and it is not allowed.

This is a good diagram below that covers it here that we can send on to them: http://download.microsoft.com/download/8/7/3/8733d036-92b0-4cb8-8912-3b6ab966b8b2/multiplexing.pdf

We should either have a meeting to explain or email them saying that apologies for the delay but we've been going back and forth with our MS licensing partner and our legal team and although it's against company policy we can make an exception in their case, but we do need them to verify their CAL usage and confirm they aren't multiplexing.

We can then either assist with buying more CAL's (we could ask for a list of user names), or just trust that they'll purchase the right amount as it's their liability.


Regards,

Solutions Architect

The licensing problem involves the product key used to install SQL Server.  Microsoft gives the exact same product key to all Software Assurance (Enterprise)/MSDN (Developer)/SPLA (Hosting) customers.  Only retail product keys are unique.  Thus, in most cases, the product key is not proof of licensing.  In past audits, if Microsoft found this product key used for SQL Server, it assumed we provided the product key and thus licensing.  If we weren’t sending Microsoft their royalty for the license, we owed back licensing fees.

 

SQL Server + CAL is a retail-only product.  It is, to my knowledge, not offered under Software Assurance, nor can it offered under SPLA.

 

If a customer buys their own SQL Server licenses through Software Assurance, they have the ability to use these licenses in Azure, other public cloud, or a hosting provider.  This is done through License Mobility and requires the license holder to submit a form attesting that they have the required license.

 

Microsoft licensing is complex and my understanding is not complete.  However, since it carries a real financial liability if we get it wrong, I believe it’s important that we understand what Microsoft allows and requires.  I’ve documents that I’ve used to make sense of this.

 

Charles Rutledge

System Specialist

Hi All,

 

Just going through the thread, checking the ticket and adding Carlton to see if he has an opinion.

 

Firstly, I don't think we are in trouble here - I think we are only responsible if we supplied them with SPLA SQL licensing and we've used the wrong number of cores or incorrect edition etc. There are no SPLA licenses on their server in Ocean, so I don't think Microsoft would even check it on an audit. If they found SQL on it we could say that the customer is using BYOL and is responsible for their own licensing (we have tickets as proof we've asked), and then they would have to produce their licenses to MS and be fined if they aren't covered correctly. @Carlton, have I got that correct?

 

Secondly, my understanding is that Charles may be incorrect (apologies Charles!), not sure if there was a recent change with the licensing models. My understanding is if this was a virtual server that can be moved between hosts, or is on a shared stack, or on our/their private cloud, or a newer version of SQL than what they are licensed for, the customer would 100% need SA on their licenses and would have to use the per core model based on the cores in the physical hosts. With Azure Arc or CSP licensing, you can now get away with just licensing the cores in the VM.

 

However, this is physical hardware dedicated to them with a Windows OS directly on top with presumably a fixed number of SQL connections, so they are allowed to use the Server license + CAL model, unless I'm missing something. @Charles was that information directly from an MS ticket? If not, we should probably open one to confirm if that model is allowed on a dedicated physical server in our data centers - I think it is now allowed.

 

In answer to the other questions, If their current licensing is confirmed as inadequate, when I compared the numbers SQL Arc would probably offer the cheapest SQL licensing for them as they can pay by the hour on their Azure consumption. If they want fixed costs, we can sell them CSP 1 year or 3 year options but the 1 year would be more expensive than Arc and the 3 year is about the same, so long as MS don't jack up their SQL PAYG pricing.

 

Regards,

 

Andy Petterson

Solutions Architect

aptum.com | LinkedIn

All,

 

I want to close the loop on this. This ticket has been open for nearly a year, it's been passed around between multiple people (several of whom are no longer here). 

 

A few things I want to clarify up front:

 

  1. This is not an Abuse issue. Abuse handles misuse of our services illegal activity like bitcoin mining. This is a commercial licensing gap. Fred TY, your team can stand down on this one. That said, we do need to clearly define what routes to Abuse vs. what's a commercial matter so we don't repeat this.
  2. Someone needs to own this. @Elizabeth Perez since you're already in communication with the customer, can you take the lead? Frederic and Barbara, please support as needed from the commercial and SDM side.
  3. The economics are what they are. Smart Scale pays us roughly $2,500/month. The compliant licensing would cost them ~$7,900/month on top of that. They're not going to pay that. Threatening suspension hasn't worked for a year and it's not going to start working now.

 

We are currently liable and at risk as an organization if we do nothing!

 

Here's what I think we should do:

 

@Elizabeth Perez, schedule a call with Mario (let's stop the back and forth email chains). He clearly doesn't understand (or doesn't want to understand) the issue, and a year of back-and-forth hasn't helped. I feel like we should present the following options:

  • They can purchase per-core licenses through us (the expensive path, don't lead with it, but it is an option)
  • They can procure Software Assurance directly from Microsoft and give us the License Mobility form (somebody to confirm... @Andy Petterson?)
  • We explore whether migrating their SQL workloads to Azure SQL makes sense, licensing is bundled in, the mobility problem goes away, and we potentially keep the relationship
    • Frederic flagged the Azure idea and I think it's worth exploring. @Andy Petterson, is it possible to get a cost comparison between the two?
  • If none of the above work, we agree on a timeline for them to move off our platform in an orderly way

 

Whatever comes out of the call, put the agreed path and deadline in writing. If they don't act, we follow through. No more open-ended limbo.

 

Let's get this resolved in the next two weeks. If anyone has questions or sees a gap in this, let me know.

 

Adam Nelson

VP, Operations

When the previous manager of Abuse (Joseph) was exited last year, a lot of historical knowledge was lost. It's also worth clarifying that he was a 1-man team; he had no reports. His Abuse responsibilities were given to InfoSec to handle as best we can, and since then, we have been trying to document some of that knowledge based on our interpretation of what Abuse should handle, which does align with what Adam said: the mis-use of our services.

 

I don't believe license (mis-)use should fall under the purview of Abuse. If a customer has installed or is using software and violating the license agreement, should Aptum be policing that at all? If we misinterpret a license agreement, couldn't that make Aptum liable? (I'm not saying Charles has misinterpreted it in this ticket, more as a general thought).

 

This ticket likely slipped through the cracks during the transition periods mentioned above, and also because, like IT, the InfoSec team don't typically look at the "Aptum" project in Jira - we have dedicated Jira projects for Security work.

Thank you for being a valued ThinkOn customer. Our team continues to work proactively to deliver a seamless and reliable service experience.

body.content:

BREAKING NEWS: Nippon Express to Acquire Metro Supply Chain for $2.2B

 

Metro Supply Chain has agreed to be acquired by Japanese global logistics giant Nippon Express Holdings in a transaction valued at up to $2.2 billion.

The deal was struck by majority shareholder LDC Metro Holdings alongside La Caisse. Founded more than 50 years ago in Montreal, Metro Supply Chain has grown into a major supply-chain solutions provider with operations across Canada, the U.S. and the U.K.

The company’s logistics real estate footprint spans 2.2 million square feet across 190 sites in North America and the U.K.

data.subject: FW: DigitalBridge Incident Escalation Policy - DRAFT

body.content:
Hi Team,
DB is rolling out a policy that requires PortCo’s to identify and report major incidents quickly and create strong governance across companies. This draft will be finalized in May/June but wanted to flag to your attention.

What must be reported: Anything with serious harm, regulatory/legal risk, reputational damage, or >$1M financial impact across areas like:

  • Safety, environment, cyber, financial fraud.
  • Operational failures, outages.
  • Legal/regulatory issues, litigation.
  • Leadership changes, misconduct, criminal activity.

 

Timing:

  • 24 hrs: Initial notification.
  • 72 hrs: Full report.

 

Notification (send to matthew.carter@aptum.com for coordination): PortCo CEO, Board, and DigitalBridge (PortfolioReporting@digitalbridge.com)

 

Requirements on p15:

  • State facts (no speculation)
  • Assess impact + regulatory status
  • Take immediate action
  • Investigate root cause + fix

 

Thanks,

 

Matthew Carter

VP, Project Management & Global Supply Chain

Hi all,

The aim of the attached draft Incident Escalation Policy is to establish clear and consistent requirements for the management and reporting of serious incidents that occur at your portfolio company. This is intended to ensure that material incidents are reported to the relevant parties through the appropriate channels.

 

We are sharing the draft policy for your review and would appreciate your support in coordinating the review within your company. Please kindly provide your feedback by April 30.

JP,

For our conversation - I had these topics/thoughts

  1. Broadcom consolidation.  We are seeing a resurgence of Broadcom with VCF 9 and our backoffice expertise is extensive.  Does it make sense to leverage that more so than trying to establish a multi-hypervisor approach.  Could this even be used across more portfolio companies?

  2. We want to accelerate our growth in the UK and does it make sense to consolidate our efforts over there into one team

  3. We've lock in storage pricing for the next 4 years PLUS we have about 8,000 hard drives that would be really strong object storage platform that we could collectively leverage include vendor co-selling

There are probably other
